title: Hot Tub and Spa Service Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Hot Tub and Spa Service Business — Costs and Earnings
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a hot tub and spa service business: real startup costs, recurring earnings, water chemistry and repair skills, and what most people get wrong.
bestFor: Hands-on people who like recurring routes, are comfortable with water chemistry, and can learn basic equipment repair
biggestRisk: Mishandling water chemistry or a repair and damaging an expensive spa, then eating the cost and losing the client
whatItIs: A hot tub and spa service business maintains residential and small commercial hot tubs and spas on a recurring basis — testing and balancing water chemistry, draining and refilling, cleaning filters and shells, treating for algae and biofilm, and handling repairs like pumps, heaters, jets, covers, and control packs. It is related to pool cleaning but genuinely distinct: spas are smaller, hotter, and far more sensitive water bodies, so chemistry swings fast and equipment problems are common. Most clients want a tech who can both maintain the water on a schedule and fix the unit when something breaks, which makes repair skill a real differentiator. Demand is steady year-round because owners use hot tubs in cold weather, and the recurring nature of the work makes it a route business with predictable monthly revenue.
dailyReality: A typical day means driving a route of scheduled stops — testing water, adding chemicals, cleaning or swapping filter cartridges, skimming and wiping shells, and checking that pumps, heaters, and jets run correctly. Recurring maintenance visits are quick (20 to 45 minutes each), so route density matters; drains-and-refills and repairs are the longer, higher-paying jobs. You will be on your knees beside tubs, handling chemicals and wet equipment, and diagnosing why a heater isn't heating or a pump is humming. Around the hands-on work you spend time scheduling, texting customers about water issues, ordering parts, and quoting repairs. Winter is busy because owners use spas most when it's cold, which is a real advantage over seasonal pool work.

startupCostsItemized.item: Reliable vehicle (use existing) plus storage bins and racks
Water testing kit / digital tester and reagents
Submersible pump, vacuum, hoses for drain-and-refill
Hand tools, multimeter, and basic repair toolkit
Starter chemical inventory (sanitizer, shock, balancers, line flush)
Diagnostic gear, spare parts inventory, and a service trailer

realEarnings.yearOne: Most operators in year one earn $1,500 to $4,000 per month part-time while building a route. Recurring maintenance commonly bills $80 to $150 per month per tub, drain-and-refills run $150 to $300, and repairs add $100 to $500-plus per job. A beginner who books 20 to 30 recurring tubs plus occasional repairs reaches the lower full-time range.
realEarnings.experienced: Operators with two-plus years, a dense route, and solid repair skills commonly report $5,000 to $9,000 per month solo. Repairs and one-time drain-and-refills are where the margin is — techs who can fix heaters, pumps, and control packs instead of just maintaining water earn meaningfully more.
realEarnings.topEarners: Multi-tech operations and shops that combine service with hot tub and parts sales gross $20,000 to $60,000-plus per month, but that requires hiring trained techs, parts inventory, a showroom or warehouse, and a shift from wrenching to managing. Most stay solo or run one or two helpers.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Route density and repair capability matter most. Tubs close together cut windshield time, and the ability to diagnose and fix equipment turns a $120/month maintenance client into a customer worth several hundred dollars more per year. Selling chemicals and filters adds margin.
realEarnings.perHour: Effective rates run $50 to $120 per hour on maintenance routes once dense, and $75 to $150-plus on repairs. Counting driving, quoting, and parts runs, realistic blended rates early on are often $40 to $80 per hour.

howToStart: Week 1: Learn spa water chemistry cold — sanitizer types (bromine vs chlorine vs salt), pH, alkalinity, calcium, and how fast hot water swings. Practice on your own or a friend's tub until you can balance confidently. Get general liability insurance before servicing any customer's spa.

Week 2: Buy a testing kit, a submersible pump for drains, basic repair tools, and a starter chemical inventory. Set simple recurring and one-time pricing. Create a Google Business Profile with clean before/after and clear-water photos.

Weeks 3-4: Land your first recurring clients through local Facebook groups, Nextdoor, and hot tub dealers who don't service what they sell. Offer a first-visit deep clean and balance to win the monthly contract.
Month 2: Build a tight route by clustering clients geographically. Learn one repair type at a time (filter and cover first, then pumps and heaters) so you can capture the higher-paying work.
Days 60-120: Add drain-and-refill and repair upsells to your maintenance base, partner with a parts supplier, and ask every happy client for a review and referral to densify your route.

mustHave: Comfort with water chemistry — understanding sanitizer, pH, alkalinity, and how hot water changes fast
Mechanical aptitude and willingness to learn equipment repair, not just water maintenance
Reliability on a recurring schedule, since clients depend on regular visits
canLearn: Diagnosing and replacing pumps, heaters, jets, control packs, and covers
Proper drain-and-refill procedure and line/biofilm flushing
Reading spa control panels and error codes across common brands (Balboa, Gecko)
separators: Repair skill — techs who fix equipment, not just balance water, earn far more per client
Route density that keeps drive time low and visits profitable
Knowing chemistry well enough to never damage a shell, equipment, or a customer's skin

whatPeopleGetWrong: Treating spa water like pool water — spas are hotter and smaller, so chemistry swings far faster and mistakes compound quickly
Only maintaining water and never learning repairs, leaving the most profitable work on the table
Damaging an expensive shell, heater, or pump through a chemistry or repair error and eating the replacement cost
Building a scattered route with long drives between tubs, which destroys the effective hourly rate
Underpricing recurring visits, then discovering chemicals, filters, and drive time eat the margin
Skipping liability insurance when a single damaged spa can cost thousands

toolsAndEquipment.item: Digital water tester and reagent kit
toolsAndEquipment.note: Accurate, fast readings are the core of the job; cheap test strips aren't enough for paid work.
For drain-and-refills, one of your higher-margin services.
Essential for diagnosing heaters, pumps, and control packs.
For jets, unions, covers, and equipment swaps.
Sanitizer, shock, balancers, and line flush; restock as routes grow.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Common spare parts (filters, heater elements, pump seals)
toolsAndEquipment.note: Carrying common parts lets you fix on the spot instead of a return trip.
Bins and racks early; a trailer only when volume justifies it.

methods: A complete Google Business Profile with clear-water and repair photos and steady reviews
Referral partnerships with hot tub dealers who sell but don't service the units
Local Facebook groups and Nextdoor, where owners ask for spa techs and complain about cloudy water
Door-to-door or mailers in neighborhoods with visible spas and outdoor decks
Asking every client for referrals and offering a discount for neighbors on the same route
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Homeowners with backyard hot tubs and swim spas, vacation-rental owners who need reliable turnover service, and small commercial spas at gyms or boutique hotels. Many bought a tub and quickly realized maintaining it is more work than expected.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Most operators land their first recurring clients within two to four weeks of marketing and build a viable route over three to six months. A dense, referral-fed route that fills the schedule usually takes one to two seasons.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Broad paid advertising and a polished brand before you have reviews and a few referral sources. Dealer relationships and local groups convert far better early on.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes. A dense recurring route plus repairs and drain-and-refills can reach full-time income within a year. The solo ceiling is set by how many stops fit in a day and your repair throughput.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible but demanding. Training techs to handle both chemistry and repairs is harder than training pure cleaners, and a bad hire can damage costly equipment. Stepping back requires documented procedures and a trustworthy lead tech.
howItScales.sellable: Yes — recurring maintenance routes with documented clients and steady repair revenue sell for a reasonable multiple, similar to pool routes. The route and contracts are the asset, not just your labor.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Standardized service and pricing, trained repair-capable techs, parts inventory, supplier relationships, and a marketing system. Adding hot tub or parts sales can multiply revenue but turns it into a retail-plus-service operation.

strongFit: You like hands-on, route-based work and recurring relationships with clients
You are comfortable with chemistry and willing to learn equipment repair
You want predictable monthly revenue rather than chasing one-off jobs
You can keep a reliable schedule, including some winter work when spas are busiest
poorFit: You dislike getting wet, kneeling beside tubs, or handling chemicals
You are not willing to learn repairs and only want to add chemicals
You want passive income or a desk-based business
You can't commit to a consistent recurring schedule
askYourself: Am I genuinely comfortable balancing water chemistry and willing to learn to fix pumps and heaters?
Is there enough hot tub density in my area to build a tight, profitable route?
Will I carry insurance and learn proper technique so I don't damage expensive equipment?

faqs.question: How is hot tub service different from pool cleaning?
faqs.answer: Spas are much smaller and hotter, so water chemistry changes far faster and is less forgiving than a pool's. Hot tubs also have more complex equipment packed into a small unit — heaters, control packs, multiple pumps and jets — so repair skill matters more. Many pool techs avoid spa work for exactly these reasons, which leaves room for specialists.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to repair equipment, or just maintain water?
faqs.answer: You can start with water maintenance alone, but repairs are where the real money is. Owners want one tech who can both balance the water and fix the heater or pump when it fails. Techs who only add chemicals leave the highest-margin work to someone else and often lose clients when a repair comes up.

faqs.question: Is hot tub service seasonal?
faqs.answer: Less than pool work. Many owners use hot tubs most in cold weather, so demand holds up year-round and can even spike in winter. That steadier, all-season demand is one of the biggest advantages of spa service over seasonal pool cleaning.

faqs.question: How much should I charge for recurring maintenance?
faqs.answer: Recurring spa maintenance commonly runs $80 to $150 per month per tub depending on visit frequency and whether chemicals are included. Drain-and-refills typically bill $150 to $300, and repairs add $100 to $500-plus. Price so that drive time, chemicals, and filters are covered, not just the time on site.

faqs.question: What's the biggest way to damage a spa?
faqs.answer: Chemistry errors — letting sanitizer or pH swing too far can corrode equipment, damage the shell, or irritate skin — and botched repairs that ruin a pump, heater, or control pack. Both are expensive to fix and can cost you the client. This is why practicing on your own tub first and carrying liability insurance are essential.

faqs.question: Can I build this into a route business I could sell?
faqs.answer: Yes. Like pool routes, a hot tub service business with documented recurring clients and steady repair revenue is a real, sellable asset. The recurring contracts and route density — not just your personal labor — are what give it value when you decide to step back or sell.

dataSources: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics — Maintenance and Repair Workers occupational data
Association of Pool and Spa Professionals (APSP/PHTA) — water chemistry and service standards
Industry cost guides (Angi, HomeAdvisor) for spa maintenance and repair pricing
Spa equipment manufacturer documentation (Balboa, Gecko) for diagnostics and parts
Operator communities and trade forums for real-world route pricing and repair earnings
